metadata.title: The Sesame Street Conspiracy
metadata.color_semantics: claude fidelity: INK #3D3929; SPARK #D97757 one accent.
metadata.author: Nik Bear Brown
metadata.credit_line: Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI
metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. An eight-week-old cannot speak but is already processing the rhythm of language around her. Music at 120 beats per minute echoes the walking pace of human locomotion. That same tempo mirrors the timing of stressed syllables in human speech. Baroque music reaches infants through the same rhythmic door by different means.

artifactLines: An eight-week-old cannot speak but is already processing the rhythm of language around her.
Music at 120 beats per minute echoes the walking pace of human locomotion.
That same tempo mirrors the timing of stressed syllables in human speech.
Baroque music reaches infants through the same rhythmic door by different means.

beats.narration_text: I'm fascinated that 120 BPM maps onto human walking pace and the rhythm of stressed syllables in speech, and that Baroque music reaches a baby differently through the same door. I want to test whether it's really the tempo doing the work. Help me: (1) list three nursery or Baroque tracks and clock their actual BPM against the 120 walking-pace benchmark; (2) design a simple at-home observation of an infant's attention at 120 versus 90 BPM; (3) explain which speech-rhythm feature the tempo is actually piggybacking on.

props.command: I'm fascinated that 120 BPM maps onto human walking pace and the rhythm of stressed syllables in speech, and that Baroque music reaches a baby differently through the same door. I want to test whether it's really the tempo doing the work. Help me: (1) list three nursery or Baroque tracks and clock their actual BPM against the 120 walking-pace benchmark; (2) design a simple at-home observation of an infant's attention at 120 versus 90 BPM; (3) explain which speech-rhythm feature the tempo is actually piggybacking on.
output: The measurement is called inter-trial phase coherence

beats.narration_text: Your eight-week-old daughter cannot speak. This is not a correlation.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Your eight-week-old daughter cannot speak. This is not a correlation.

beats.narration_text: It’s not that they can’t process language. Baroque music achieves something similar through different means.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: It’s not that they can’t process language. Baroque music achieves something similar through different means.

beats.narration_text: In the stressed syllables of human speech. In the walking pace of human locomotion.

beats.narration_text: Written by Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI. Humanitarians AI is an educational bridge program where Fellows learn experiential AI by doing AI — building real projects and shipping real research.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Paste this into Claude — Take the central idea from 'The Sesame Street Conspiracy' and give me the three most actionable takeaways — one sentence each.… That's the prompt that actually tests whether you got it. Run it and see where it surprises you.

props.command: Take the central idea from 'The Sesame Street Conspiracy' and give me the three most actionable takeaways — one sentence each. Then name the failure mode I'm most likely to hit.
